question: Match the following :
explanation:
The block to which an element belongs can be determined by identifying the orbital to which the last electron enters.
$(\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{Q}){ }_{88} \mathrm{Ra}=[\mathrm{Rn}] 7 \mathrm{~s}^{2}$. The last electron in s shell. So, it is s-block element.
$(\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{P}){ }_{114}$ Uuq $=[\mathrm{Rn}] 7 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 5 \mathrm{f}^{14} 6 \mathrm{~d}^{10} 7 \mathrm{p}^{2}$. So, it is p-block element.
$(\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{S}){ }_{110} \mathrm{Ds}=[\mathrm{Rn}] 7 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 5 \mathrm{f}^{14} 6 \mathrm{~d}^{8}$. So, it is a d-block element.
(D-R) ${ }_{100} \mathrm{Fm}=[\mathrm{Rn}] 7 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 5 \mathrm{f}^{12}$. So, it is a f-block element.

options.text: A - Q, 	B - R, 	C - S, 	   D - P
A - R, 	B - Q, 	C - P, 	   D - S
A - R, 	B - S, 		C - Q, 	   D - P
A - Q,  	B - P, 		C - S, 	   D - R

question: The set of elements which obey the general electronic configuration  $({\text{n}} - 1)\,{{\text{d}}^{1 - 10}}\,{\text{n}}{{\text{s}}^2}$   is
explanation:
${ }_{108} \mathrm{Hs}:[\mathrm{Rn}] 5 \mathrm{f}^{14} 6 \mathrm{~d}^{6} 7 \mathrm{~s}^{2}$
${ }_{80} \mathrm{Hg}:[\mathrm{Xe}] 4 \mathrm{f}^{14} 5 \mathrm{~d}^{10} 6 \mathrm{~s}^{2}$
${ }_{74} \mathrm{~W}:[\mathrm{Xe}] 4 \mathrm{f}^{14} 5 \mathrm{~d}^{4} 6 \mathrm{~s}^{2}$
Hs, Hg, W elements have general electronic configuration $(\mathrm{n}-1) \mathrm{d}^{1-10} \mathrm{~ns}^{2}$.

question: Match the following groups of the periodic table with their general outer electronic configuration. Choose the correct option:
explanation:
(A - S) Group 1 elements, also known as alkali metals, have a single electron in their outermost s-orbital represented by $n{s^1}.$
(B - R) Group 2 elements, or alkaline earth metals, have two electrons in their outermost s-orbital, represented by ${\rm{n}}{{\rm{s}}^2}.$
(C - Q) Transition metals have a partially filled d subshell, $({\rm{n}} - 1)\,{{\rm{d}}^{1 - 10}}\,{\rm{n}}{{\rm{s}}^{0 - 2}}.$
(D - P) Inner transition metals have a partially filled f subshell, $({\rm{n}} - 2){{\rm{f}}^{1 - 14}}({\rm{n}} - 1)\,{{\rm{d}}^{0 - 1}}\,{\rm{n}}{{\rm{s}}^2}$ which corresponds to the last electrons being added to these orbitals. Correct option is (4).

options.text: A - Q,     B - P, 	C - R, 	D - S
A - P,      B - Q, 	C - R, 	D - S
A - R,     B - S, 	C - P, 	D - Q
A - S,     B - R, 	C - Q, 	D - P

question: Match the different block elements with their general electronic configurations. Choose the correct option:
explanation:
(A - R) s-block elements have the outer electronic configuration ending in ${\rm{n}}{{\rm{s}}^1}$ or ${\rm{n}}{{\rm{s}}^2}.$
(B - P) p-block elements have their valence shell configuration ranging from ${\rm{n}}{{\rm{s}}^2}{\rm{n}}{{\rm{p}}^1}$ to ${\rm{n}}{{\rm{s}}^2}{\rm{n}}{{\rm{p}}^6}.$
(C - S) d-block elements typically have an (n1)d subshell that is being filled,
(D - Q) f - block elements have an (n - 2) f subshell that is being filled. Correct option is (1).

options.text: A - R,    B - P, 	C - S, 	D - Q
A - Q,   B - P, 	C - R, 	D - S

question: Match the following types of elements with their characteristic general electronic configurations. Choose the correct option:
explanation:
(A - R) Alkali metals have a single electron in their outermost s-orbital, ${\rm{n}}{{\rm{s}}^1}.$
(B - S) Alkaline earth metals have two electrons in the outermost s-orbital ${\rm{n}}{{\rm{s}}^2}.$
(C - P) Halogens have five electrons in their outermost p-orbital ${\rm{n}}{{\rm{s}}^2}{\rm{n}}{{\rm{p}}^5}$ aiming to complete their octet.
(D - Q) Noble gases have a complete octet ${\rm{n}}{{\rm{s}}^2}{\rm{n}}{{\rm{p}}^6}$ in their outermost p orbital. Correct option is (3).

explanation:
(A - Q) Alkali metals have a valence shell electronic configuration of ${\text{n}}{{\text{s}}^1}.$
(B - R) Lanthanoids have a filling of 4 f orbitals.
(C - S) Actinoids have a filling of 5f orbitals.
(D - P) Noble gases have completely filled valence shells, which gives them unique stability. Correct option is (2).

question: Match the following terms related to the structure of the periodic table with their correct descriptions. Choose the correct option:
explanation:
(A - S) A period in the periodic table corresponds to a horizontal row.
(B - R) A group contains elements with similar valence shell electronic configurations.
(C - Q) The modern periodic law states that the properties of elements are periodic functions of their atomic numbers.
(D - P) Transition elements involve the filling of d-orbitals. Correct option is (4).

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
Actinoids exhibit more variable oxidation states than lanthanoids.
The energy difference between 5f, 6d, and 7s orbitals allows for a variety of electron configurations in actinoids.
explanation: Actinoids have a small energy difference between the 5f, 6d, and 7s orbitals, which allows electrons to be lost from any of these,   leading to multiple oxidation states. Lanthanoids have  a  less pronounced energy difference and exhibit fewer oxidation states.   Correct option is (1).

question: Which element does not exist in elemental state in the earth's crust?
explanation: Out of given four elements $\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{S}, \mathrm{Fe}$ and Au the non-metals C and S exist in elemental form as allotropes. Au is a less active metal. It also exists in nature in elemental form, called nugget. Fe is an electropositive metal. So, it exists in earth's crust in combined state (compounds), e.g. $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}, \mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}, \mathrm{FeS}_{2}$ etc.

question:
Which among the following electronic configurations belong to main group elements?
A. $[{\text{Ne}}]\,3{{\text{s}}^1}$
B. $[{\text{Ar}}]\,3{{\text{d}}^3}4{{\text{s}}^2}$
C. $[{\text{Kr}}]\,4{{\text{d}}^{10}}5\,{{\text{s}}^2}5{{\text{p}}^5}$
D. $[{\text{Ar}}]\,3{{\text{d}}^{10}}4{{\text{s}}^1}$
E. $[{\text{Rn}}]{\mkern 1mu} \,5{{\text{f}}^0}6{{\text{d}}^2}7{{\text{s}}^2}$
Choose the correct answer from the option given below :
explanation:
(A) $[\mathrm{Ne}] 3 \mathrm{~s}^{1}$ : Na (s-block)
(B) $[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{3} 4 \mathrm{~s}^{2}: \mathrm{V}$ (d-block)
(C) $[\mathrm{Kr}] 4 \mathrm{~d}^{10} 5 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 5 \mathrm{p}^{5}:(\mathrm{p}$-block)
(D) $[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{10} 4 \mathrm{~s}^{1}: \mathrm{Cu}$ (d-block)
(E) $[\mathrm{Rn}] 5 \mathrm{f}^{0} 6 \mathrm{~d}^{2} 7 \mathrm{~s}^{2}$ : Th (f-block)
Main group elements are the elements of s and p-block. Thus, only 'A' and 'C' belong to this category.

question: Match Column I with Column II and choose the correct combination from the options given.
explanation:
(A - R, S)
$[\mathrm{Xe}] 4 \mathrm{f}^{14} 5 \mathrm{~d}^{2} 6 \mathrm{~s}^{2}$ : Last $\mathrm{e}^{-}$enters to 5d subshell so it is d- block element and 5 d subshell is partially filled, so it is also considered transition element.
(B - Q, T)
[Xe] $4 \mathrm{f}^{14} 5 \mathrm{~d}^{10} 6 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 6 \mathrm{p}^{6} 7 \mathrm{~s}^{2}$ : Last $\mathrm{e}^{-}$enters in 7 s subshell, so it is s-block and representative element.
(C - P)
$[\mathrm{Rn}] 5 \mathrm{f}^{14} 6 \mathrm{~d}^{1} 7 \mathrm{~s}^{2}$ : Last $\mathrm{e}^{-}$enters to 5 f subshell so it is f-block and inner-transition element.
(D - R)
[Xe] $4 \mathrm{f}^{14} 5 \mathrm{~d}^{10} 6 \mathrm{~s}^{2}$ : Last $\mathrm{e}^{-}$enters to 5 d subshell so it is d -block element and 5d sub-shell is full filled so it is not considered as transition element.

question: The electronic configuration of the element which is just above the element with atomic number 43 in the same group is
explanation:
The element with $Z=43$ lies in the 5 th period. Since, the 4th period has 18 elements, therefore, the atomic number of the element which lies immediately above the element with atomic number 43 is $43-18=25$.
Now, the electronic configuration of element with $\mathrm{Z}=25$ is $1 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{p}^{6} 3 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 3 \mathrm{p}^{6} 3 \mathrm{~d}^{5} 4 \mathrm{~s}^{2}$

question:
How many of the following statements are correct?
(A) 'He' is the second most abundant element in the universe.
(B) The symbol for the element with atomic number 110 is Ds.
(C) Osmium has the highest density among all elements.
(D) Francium is the most electropositive element in the periodic table.
explanation:
Helium (He) is the second-most abundant element in the universe, after hydrogen. Darmstadtium is a chemical element with the symbol Ds and atomic number 110. The very small atomic radius of osmium results in a small metal-metal separation. This small atomic separation along with osmium's relatively high atomic number gives rise to osmium's highest density.
In group IA, Francium is more electropositive than cesium but, not stable as it is highly radioactive (maximum half-life of only 22 minutes).

question:
(i) $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O} < \mathrm{MgO} < \mathrm{ZnO} < \mathrm{P}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{6}$-Acidic property
(ii) $\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{Cl}>\mathrm{Br}$-Electron gain enthalpy
(iii) $\mathrm{M}^{2-}>\mathrm{M}^{-}>\mathrm{M}^{+}>\mathrm{M}^{2+}$ - ionic size
(iv) The second ionisation enthalpy of Cu is more than second ionisation enthalpy of K .
Which of the following is the correct representation of True (T) / False (F) for the given statements?
explanation:
(i) Acidic nature increases along the period (according to long form of the periodic table).
So, statement (i) is true.
(ii) Electron gain enthalpy for Cl is more $(-)$ ve than of F (due to extremely small size of F $ - $ atom) and electron gain enthalpy for Br $ - $ atom is less $(-)$ ve than of $\mathrm{Cl}-$ atom (due to its larger size). Hence, the statement (ii) is false.
(iii) For same element, ionic radii : anionic > neutral > cationic.
$\mathrm{M}^{2-}>\mathrm{M}^{-}>\mathrm{M}^{+}>\mathrm{M}^{2+}$
Thus, this statement (iii) is true.
(iv) For $2^{\text {nd }}$ I.E, the electron is removed from
$\mathrm{M}^{+}$ion. $\mathrm{K}^{+}$ has fully-filled noble gas
configuration while $\mathrm{Cu}^{+}$has full-filled $\mathrm{d}^{10}$ configuration and K has no d -subshell.
Thus, $2^{\text {nd }}$ I.E for $\mathrm{K}^{+}$is more than of $\mathrm{Cu}^{+}$ ion.
Hence, statement (iv) is false.

options.text: i - T      ii - T       iii - F         iv - F
i - F      ii - T       iii - F         iv - T
i - F      ii - F       iii - F         iv - T
i - T      ii - F       iii - T         iv - F

${\mathrm{\sqrt{v}=a(Z-b)}}$
(or) ${\mathrm{\sqrt{v} \propto Z}}$
So, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{n}=\dfrac{1}{2}}}$

question:
Assertion: Elements in the same vertical column have similar properties.

Reason: Elements have periodic dependence upon the atomic number.
explanation: Elements within the same group (vertical column) in the periodic table have the same number of electrons in their outermost energy level, and consequently, they share similar electronic configurations. As a result, these elements exhibit similar chemical properties and behaviors. So option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question: What should be the atomic number of the next halogen if discovered in future?
explanation: The next halogen will have ${\rm{7}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}\,{\rm{7}}{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{5}}}$ outer configuration. Since, the filling of 7p-orbitals will begin after 5f and 6d-orbitals, thus the atomic number of the new halogen will be 112 (up to the filling of 6d-orbitals)plus 5, i.e., 117.

question: Match the following IUPAC temporary names of elements (based on atomic numbers) with their corresponding discovered names. Choose the correct option:
explanation:
Correct match is as follows:
(A - R) Unnilunium (IUPAC system name for element 101) is officially named Mendelevium.
(B - Q) Unnilhexium (IUPAC system name for element 106) is officially named Seaborgium.
(C - S) Unnilennium (IUPAC system name for element 109) is officially named Meitnerium.
(D - P) Unnilbium (IUPAC system name for element 102) is officially named Nobelium. Correct option is (2).

Assertion: Elements with atomic numbers greater than 100 are given a temporary systematic nomenclature until their names are officially recognized.

Reason: The IUPAC recommends this systematic nomenclature to avoid controversies in the naming of new elements and to provide a standardized method for referring to these elements.
explanation: The systematic nomenclature is based on the atomic number of elements using numerical roots for 0 and numbers (1-9), allowing for a unique and systematic way to refer to new elements until a permanent name is ratified by the IUPAC. Correct option is (1).

question:
Assertion: Atomic number of the element copernicium is 112.

Reason: IUPAC name of this element is ununbium in which un and bi-are used for 1 and 2 respectively in latin words.
explanation:
The IUPAC name of the element with atomic number 112 is "copernicium."
According to systematic nomenclature root words are put together in order of digits which make up the atomic number and "ium" is added at the end. 1 is reffered as 'un' and 2 as 'bi'. Therefore, 112 is Ununbium. Thus option (2) is correct.

question: Among the oxides $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}, \mathrm{SO}_{2}, \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$, the correct order of acidic strength is
explanation:
Oxides of non-metal are more acidic. Across the period non-metallic character increases and thus the acidic nature of oxides also increases. Hence, the correct order is,
$\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} < \mathrm{SiO}_{2} < \mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} < \mathrm{SO}_{2}$.

question:
Which one among the following statements is/ are not correct?
I. Ba compounds, generally are more covalent than Be compounds.
II. The electron gain enthalpy of He is positive.
III. The oxidation states of O in $\mathrm{OF}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ are same.
IV. The radius of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ion is smaller than that of $\mathrm{F}^{-}$ion.
explanation:
The explanation of given statements are:
I. Both beryllium ( Be ) and barium ( Ba ) belongs to the same group (i.e. group-II) and as we move down the group, size of atom/ion increases while their covalent character decreases. Thus, Ba compounds are generally less covalent than Be compounds.
Therefore, the given statement is not correct.
II. The electron gain enthalpy of He is positive because of very small size with full-filled electronic configuration. (i.e. 1s²). Hence, the given statement is correct.
III. The oxidation state of O in $\mathrm{OF}_{2}=+1$ and oxidation state of O in $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}=-2$. Hence, the given statement is not correct.
IV. The radius of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ion is smaller than $\mathrm{F}^{-}$ion because both are isoelectronic species but $\mathrm{F}^{-}$ has less number of protons than, that of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$. Hence, $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ion is of smaller in size than that of $\mathrm{F}^{-}$ion (due to greater attractive forces in sodium). Thus, I and III are not the correct statements.
Hence, option (4) is the correct answer.
subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

question:
Read the statement A and statement B to mark the correct option given below.
Along the period, the chemical reactivity of the elements gradually increases from group 1 to group 18.
The nature of oxides formed by group 1 elements is basic while that of group 17 elements is acidic.
explanation: Chemical reactivity of the elements is high at the two extremes (i.e., group 1 and group 17) and the lowest in the centre). The nature of oxides formed by group, 1 elements is basic while that of group 17 elements is acidic. ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}$ (basic) and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}}}$ (acidic). So, the correct option is (2).

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

question: The diagonal relationship in Be and Al is due to
explanation: The diagonal relationship in Be and Al is due to similar ionic size and charge/radius ratio. Generally, on moving from left to right across a period, the ionic charge increases to maximum and then decreases, while the ionic size decreases, causing an increase in its polarising power (Fajan’s rule). On the other hand, on moving down a group the ionic charge remains the same while ionic size increases. Therefore, polarising power decreases. On moving diagonally, these two effects partly balance each other and therefore, there is no marked change in their properties.

options.text: similar ionic size and charge/radius ratio

question:

The electron gain enthalpy is a measure of the energy change when an electron is added to a neutral atom.
Ionization enthalpy is the energy required to remove the most loosely bound electron from an isolated gaseous atom.
explanation: Both statements are correct definitions of electron gain enthalpy and ionization enthalpy, respectively, which are critical in understanding the process of ionic bond formation. Correct option is (1)

question: Which of the following has the least electron gain enthalpy?
explanation: Halogens like chlorine and iodine have higher electron gain enthalpies due to seven valence electrons, lacking only one for a complete octet. Among oxygen and sulphur, sulphur has  a higher electron gain enthalpy than oxygen due to larger size and greater effective nuclear charge. Thus, among the given options, oxygen has the least electron gain enthalpy.

question: Identify the pair of elements in which the difference in atomic radii is maximum.
explanation:
(1) C and N both element are in same period. Carbon has atomic radius approximately 70 pm , while nitrogen has 65 pm . So, the difference is $(70-65)=5 \mathrm{pm}$.
(2) O and F are in the same period. Oxygen atomic radius is 60 pm . Fluorine has atomic radius approximately 50 pm . So, the difference is $(60-50)=10 \mathrm{pm}$.
(3) P has atomic radius of approximately 100 pm, while sulfur has atomic radius 100 pm .
So, there will be no difference.
(4) Li has atomic radius of approximately 155 pm while Be has atomic Radius of 105 pm . So,
the difference is $(155-105)=50 \mathrm{pm}$. Li and Be have the maximum difference in radii.

question: If first ionization enthalpy $\left(\Delta_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{H}\right)$ values of $\mathrm{Na}, \mathrm{Mg}$ and Si are respectively 496,737 and $786 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, the first ionization enthalpy value of Al (in  ${\text{kJ}}\,\,{\text{mo}}{{\text{l}}^{ - 1}})$  will be
explanation:
Ionization energy depends on the size, effective nuclear charge and electronic configuration. It increases across a period. Mg has fully filled s orbital so its ionization energy is more than aluminium.
Hence, the correct order of $\Delta_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{H}$ values is,
$\mathrm{Si}>\mathrm{Mg}>\mathrm{Al}>\mathrm{Na}$
$\begin{array}{llll}786 & 737 & 575 & 496\end{array}$
So, the value of Al is in between the range of 737 to 496.

Boron has a lower first ionization energy than Beryllium.
Boron's outer electron is in a p - orbital, which is higher in energy and easier to remove than the s-orbital electron in Beryllium.
explanation: The first ionization energy of Boron is lower than that of Beryllium even though Boron has a higher atomic number, due to the added electron being in a (2 p)-orbital, which has a higher energy level and experiences less nuclear attraction compared to the 2 s -orbital electron in Beryllium.    Correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
Alkali metals have lower ionization energies compared to alkaline earth metals in the same period.
Alkali metals have a single electron in their outermost s-orbital, which is easier to remove compared to the two electrons in the s-orbital of alkaline earth metals.
explanation: Alkali metals have a single electron in their outermost s-orbital, making it easier to remove due to less electron-electron repulsion, resulting in lower ionization energies compared to the more tightly bound pair of electrons in alkaline earth metals.     Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct options given below:

Assertion: Alkali metals have low electron gain enthalpies.

Reason: Alkali metals have a single electron in their outermost shell, which is loosely held and easily lost.
explanation: While it is true that alkali metals have low electron gain enthalpies, the reason is not because they lose an electron easily (which relates to ionization energy), but because they have a low tendency to gain electrons due to their large atomic radii and low effective nuclear charge. Correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and 	 Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but 	 Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Assertion is  false  but reason is  true.

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct options given below:

Assertion: Electronegativity decreases down a group in the periodic table.

Reason: An increase in atomic radius and additional inner shells of electrons down a group reduce the nuclear attraction experienced by the valence electrons.
explanation: The farther away the valence shell is from the nucleus and the more shielded by inner electrons, the less capable an atom is towards attracting additional electrons, thus decreasing electronegativity. Correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and 	 Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but 	 Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct options given below:

Assertion: The ionization energy of elements increases across a period.

Reason: Additional electrons are added to the same energy level while protons are added to the nucleus, increasing the attraction of electrons to the nucleus across a period.
explanation: Across a period, the increased nuclear charge due to additional protons causes the electrons to be held more tightly, thereby increasing the ionization energy. Correct option is (1).

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct options given below:

Assertion: Oxygen has a higher electronegativity than nitrogen.

Reason: Oxygen has more protons than nitrogen, leading to a greater effective nuclear charge and higher electronegativity.
explanation: Oxygen's higher atomic number means a greater nuclear charge, which attracts bonding pairs of electrons more strongly, making it more electronegative than nitrogen.   Correct option is (1).

Assertion: Fluorine has a higher electron gain enthalpy than oxygen.

Reason: Fluorine has a smaller atomic radius than oxygen, which leads to greater electron electron repulsions when an electron is added.
explanation: Although fluorine does have a higher electron gain enthalpy than oxygen, the reason is not greater electron-electron repulsions due to smaller atomic radius but rather fluorine's   high electronegativity and the ability to attain a noble gas configuration upon gaining an electron. Correct option is (3).

Assertion: Ionization energy decreases down a group.

Reason: The outermost electrons are more shielded and are farther from the nucleus as the atomic size increases.
explanation: In larger atoms, the outer electrons are not only farther away from the nucleus but are also shielded by more inner electrons, making it easier to remove an electron and hence, the ionization energy is lower. Correct option is (1).

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct options given below:

Assertion: The atomic radius decreases across a period.

Reason: An increase in the effective nuclear charge across a period pulls the electrons closer to the nucleus.
explanation: As the atomic number increases across a period, the effective nuclear charge that electrons feel also increases because of a greater number of protons in the nucleus. This stronger pull decreases the atomic radius. Correct option is (1).

Assertion: The elements with higher atomic numbers in a period tend to have higher ionization energies.

Reason: In a period as the atomic number increases, the additional electrons enter the same energy level and are held more tightly by the increased nuclear charge, thus increasing the ionization energy.
explanation: Within a given period, as the atomic number increases, the nuclear charge also increases. The valence electrons are added to the same energy level and they experience a greater effective nuclear charge, resulting in a higher ionization energy.  Correct option is (1).

question:
Consider the following.
(I) The order of first ionisation enthalpy of first three elements of $3^{\text {rd }}$ period is $\mathrm{Mg}>\mathrm{Al}>\mathrm{Na}$.
(II) The element with electronegativity of 3.5 is chlorine.
(III) The order of sizes of ions $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{F}^{-}$ and $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ is $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+} < \mathrm{Na}^{+} < \mathrm{F}^{-} < \mathrm{O}^{2-}$.
(IV) The IUPAC name of the element with atomic number 106 is bohrium.
The correct statements are
explanation: Among the given statements, I and III only are correct while that in II and IV are incorrect. It is because the electronegativity of chlorine is 3 not 3.5. Also, the IUPAC name of element with atomic number 106 is unnilhexium.

question: In which of the following options, the elements are correctly arranged with respect to their negative electron gain enthalpies?
explanation: The electron gain enthalpy (EA) increases along a period and decreases down the group. So, elements of group 17 i.e., Cl and F have more  $( - {\text{ve}})$  EA than $16^{\text {th }}$ group element i.e., S has more  $( - {\text{ve}})$  EA than $15^{\text {th }}$ group element P. However, EA of F is less than Cl due to small size of fluorine.Therefore, correct order of electron affinity is $\mathrm{Cl}>\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{S}>\mathrm{P}$.

question: Choose the correct order of second ionisation enthalpies of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and fluorine.
explanation:
The correct order of second ionisation enthalpy is oxygen $>$ fluorine $>$ nitrogen $>$ carbon. Electronic configuration of elements after removal of first electron,
$\mathrm{O}^{+}=1 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{p}^{3} ; \mathrm{F}^{+}=1 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{p}^{4}$
$\mathrm{N}^{+}=1 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{p}^{2} ; \mathrm{C}^{+}=1 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{p}^{1}$
The second I.E. of oxygen is more, as it acquires a half-filled stable electronic configuration. So, require more energy to remove electron from oxygen. Carbon has least I.E. as it will remove one electron to attain a stable fully-filled configuration.

question: The correct order of ionic radii for the given species is
explanation:
The correct order of ionic radii is $\mathrm{K}^{+}>\mathrm{Na}^{+}>\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}>\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$. In this $\mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ are isoelectronic species so in these size decreases with rising atomic number because on rising atomic number ${{\text{Z}}_{{\text{eff }}}}$ increases ( ${{\text{Z}}_{{\text{eff}}}} = $ effective nuclear charge).
Also, $\mathrm{K}^{+}$has the highest ionic radii as it belongs to fourth period and has highest number of shells.

question:
The change in enthalpy $[\Delta \mathrm{H}]$ in $\mathrm{kJmol}^{-1}$ for the reaction, $\mathrm{Mg}+2 \mathrm{~F} \rightarrow \mathrm{MgF}_{2}$ is
Given, electron affinity of $\mathrm{F}=328 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$, $\mathrm{IE}_{1}$ of
$\mathrm{Mg}=737 \mathrm{kJmol}^{-1}, \mathrm{IE}_{2}$ of $\mathrm{Mg}=1451 \mathrm{kJmol}^{-1}$
explanation:
$\mathrm{Mg} \rightarrow \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}=\mathrm{IE}_{1}+\mathrm{IE}_{2}$
$2 \mathrm{~F}+2 e^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~F}^{-} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}_{2}=2 \mathrm{EA}_{1}$
$\mathrm{Mg}+2 \mathrm{~F}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{~F}^{-} ; \Delta \mathrm{H}=$ ?
$\Delta \mathrm{H}=\Delta \mathrm{H}_{1}+\Delta \mathrm{H}_{2}=737+1451+2(-328)$
$=1532 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$.

question: Arrange the following in increasing order of ionic radii $\mathrm{O}^{2-}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{F}^{-}, \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$
explanation:
In isoelectronic series, the species which has maximum negative charge will have maximum ionic radii and species which have maximum positive charge will have minimum ionic radii. Thus order of ionic radii will be
$\mathrm{O}^{2-}>\mathrm{F}^{-}>\mathrm{Na}^{+}>\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$

question: Which of the following sequence is correct for decreasing order of ionic radius?
explanation: The size of the ion depends on number of shells, more are the number of shells, larger is the size of anion. When number of shells are same (like in $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ and $\mathrm{F}^{-}$), more negative ion will have larger size. Therefore, the correct decreasing order of ionic radius will be$\mathrm{I}^{-}>\mathrm{Se}^{2-}>\mathrm{Br}^{-}>\mathrm{O}^{2-}>\mathrm{F}^{-}$

question: The electronegativity order of the following elements is
explanation: Electronegativity is inversely proportional to the atomic size. Smaller the size, greater is the electronegativity. Nitrogen is placed in second period, so it has smallest size and highest electronegativity. Then, iodine belongs to halogen family which are electronegative elements. For, S and Te , on moving top to bottom, atomic size increases so electronegative character decreases. So, the correct order is $\mathrm{N}>\mathrm{I}>\mathrm{S}>\mathrm{Te}$.

question: Which of the following statements is incorrect?
explanation: Enthalpy of atomisation is the energy required to break off atoms, which are joined together. From top to bottom in a group, the atomic radii increases due to addition of extra electron shells. Atoms with larger radii attract each other more weakly than atoms with smaller radii, so it is easier to separate the larger size atoms by the less input of energy and hence on moving down a group ( s -block and p-block) have low atomisation enthalpy. But in the case of d -block elements enthalpy of atomisation increases on moving down a group due to presence of lanthanide and actinides contractions. Hence, correct option is (3).

options.text: The enthalpy of atomisation decreases down a group in s-block elements
The enthalpy of atomisation decreases down a group in p-block elements
The enthalpy of atomisation decreases down a group in d-block elements
The enthalpy of atomisation increases down a group in d-block elements

question:
Read the following Assertion & Reason Mark correct options given below.

Assertion: $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ are isoelectronic but the magnitude of ionic radius of $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ is less than that in $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$.

Reason: The effective nuclear charge on the outermost electrons in $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ is greater than that in $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$.
explanation:
Higher the electrostatic attraction between nucleus and valence electron, smaller will be the size of the atom/ion. Thus, $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ has lower ionic radii than $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ due to higher effective nuclear charge.
Hence, Assertion is true, Reason is true and Reason is the correct explanation for Assertion.

question:
The successive ionisation energy values for an element ' X ' are given below:
(i) ${1^{{\text{st}}}}$ ionisation energy $=410 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
(ii) $2^{\text {nd }}$ ionisation energy $=820 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
(iii) $3^{\text {rd }}$ ionisation energy $=1100 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
(iv) $4^{\text {th }}$ ionisation energy $=1500 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
(v) $5^{\text {th }}$ ionisation energy $=3200 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
The number valence electrons of element ‘X’ are
explanation: Maximum difference is between the fifth and fourth ionisation energy (Removal of the $5^{\text {th }}$ electron requires almost more than double the energy required to remove the $4^{\text {th }}$ electron). This implies that the first four electrons can be removed from the valence shell. Hence, number of valence electrons in the atom ' X ' will be four.

question: What is the correct order of atomic/ionic size?
explanation:
Atomic/ionic radii increases down the group and decreases across a period. Neutral atom has larger radii than its cation.
$\therefore$ The correct order of radii is
$\mathrm{Zn}^{+2} < \mathrm{Cu}^{+} < \mathrm{Cu} < \mathrm{Ag}$.
subject: Chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
topic: Periodic Trends in Periodic Properties of Elements
parent_topic: Periodic Trends in Periodic Properties of Elements

question:
Which of the following are correct?
(i) First ionisation enthalpy of $\mathrm{He} < $ Second ionisation enthalpy of Li .
(ii) Li has the highest second ionisation enthalpy.
(iii) All d-block elements are transition elements.
(iv) The only alphabet not found in the periodic table is the letter ' J '.
(v) Francium concentration is $\sim 10^{-18} \mathrm{ppm}$ on Earth.
explanation:
All transition elements are d-block elements but all d-block elements are not transition metals. A transition metal is one which forms one or more stable ions which have incomplete d - orbitals. On the basis of this definition, scandium and zinc do not count as transition metals although they are d-block elements.
Thus, statement (iii) is incorrect.
subject: Chemistry

topic: Periodic Trends in Periodic Properties of Elements

question: The correct increasing order of ionisation enthalpy of $\mathrm{He}, \mathrm{Li}^{+}, \mathrm{Be}^{2+}$ is
explanation:
$\mathrm{He}, \mathrm{Li}^{+}, \mathrm{Be}^{+2}$ are isoelectric.
Ionisation potential $\propto$ nuclear charge
$\therefore$ The correct order of I.P. is $\mathrm{He}^{2} < \mathrm{Li}^{+} < \mathrm{Be}^{+2}$

question:
Which of the following statements are true?
A. Unlike Ga that has a very high melting point, Cs has a very low melting point.
B. On Pauling scale, the electronegativity values of N and Cl are not the same.
C. $\mathrm{Ar}, \mathrm{K}^{+}, \mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$, and $\mathrm{S}^{2-}$ are all isoelectronic species.
D. The correct order of the first ionization enthalpies of $\mathrm{Na}, \mathrm{Mg}, \mathrm{Al}$, and Si is $\mathrm{Si}>\mathrm{Al}>\mathrm{Mg}>\mathrm{Na}$.
E. The atomic radius of Cs is greater than that of Li and Rb .
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
explanation:
(A) Both Ga and Cs have low melting points.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956322/preptracker/questions/q_103796_explanation_1764956321804.png
(B) On Pauling scale, the electronegativity value of N and Cl have same (3.0).
(C) Ar, $\mathrm{K}^{+}, \mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{S}^{2-}$ have 18 electrons each. So these are isoelectronic species.
(D) The correct order of first ionization enthalpy is $\mathrm{Si}>\mathrm{Mg}>\mathrm{Al}>\mathrm{Na}$
Mg has higher ionization enthalpy than AI due to better penetration power of the s electron than the p electron.
(E) Atomic radii increases down the group. Cs is the largest atom.
So, C and E are correct statements only.

$\mathrm{Al}^{3+} < \mathrm{Mg}^{2+} < \mathrm{Na}^{+} < \mathrm{F}^{-}$

question:
Consider the following elements. Which of the following is/are true about ${\mathrm{\mathrm{A}^{\prime}, \mathrm{B}^{\prime}, \mathrm{C}^{\prime}}}$ and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{D}^{\prime}}}$ ?
(I) Order of atomic radii : ${\mathrm{\mathrm{B}^{\prime} < \mathrm{A}^{\prime} < \mathrm{D}^{\prime} < \mathrm{C}^{\prime}}}$
(II) Order of metallic character :${\mathrm{B^{\prime} < A^{\prime} < D^{\prime} < C^{\prime}}}$
(III) Size of the elements: ${\mathrm{\mathrm{D}^{\prime} < \mathrm{C}^{\prime} < \mathrm{B}^{\prime} < \mathrm{A}^{\prime}}}$
(IV) Order of ionic radii : ${{\rm{B}}^{\prime {\rm{ + }}}} < {{\rm{A}}^{^{\prime {\rm{ + }}}}} < {{\rm{D}}^{\prime {\rm{ + }}}} < {{\rm{C}}^{\prime {\rm{ + }}}}$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
explanation:
In the periodic table, the size, atomic and ionic radii of an element increase down the group and decrease across a period. Metallic nature increases down the group and decreases across a period from left to right.
So, the correct option is (1).

question:
The correct sequence of electron gain enthalpy of the elements listed below is
${\rm{(I)Ar}}\,\,\,{\rm{(II)Br}}\quad {\rm{(III)F}}\,\,\,\,{\rm{(IV)S}}$
Choose the most appropriate from the options given below
explanation:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{F}, \mathrm{Br}, \mathrm{S}}}$ have $-$ve electron gain enthalpy whereas Ar has +ve electron gain enthalpy.
${\rm{Element}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\rm{Electron}}\,{\rm{gain}}\,{\rm{enthalpy[in}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\rm{kJ/mol]}}$
${\rm{F}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, - {\rm{333}}$
${\rm{Br}}\quad \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, - {\rm{325}}$
${\rm{S}}\quad \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, - {\rm{200}}$
${\rm{Ar}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{ + 96}}$
Based on the sign, the correct order is
I ${\mathrm{>}}$ IV ${\mathrm{>}}$ III ${\mathrm{>}}$ II.
So, the correct option is (1).

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason to mark the correct option given below.

Assertion: There is a considerable increase in covalent radius from N to P . However from As to Bi only a small increase in covalent radius is observed.

Reason: Covalent and ionic radii in a particular oxidation state increase in down the group
explanation:
Small increase in covalent radii from As to Bi is due to poor shielding effect of d - and f electrons present in the inner shells on valence electrons. As a result, effective nuclear charge increases, which does not allow much increase in radius.
So, given both statements are correct but reason is not the explanation for assertion. So, the correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
The statement(s) that are correct about the species ${\mathrm{\mathrm{O}^{2-}, \mathrm{F}^{-}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}}}$and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}}}$.
(I) All are isoelectronic.
(II) All have the same nuclear charge.
(III) ${\mathrm{\mathrm{O}^{2-}}}$ has the largest ionic radii.
(IV) ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}}}$ has the smallest ionic radii.
explanation:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{O}^{2-}, \mathrm{F}^{-}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}}}$and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}}}$ all have 10 electrons so they are isoelectronic. The number of protons in nucleus is different hence, their nuclear charge is not same. Order of radii is
${\mathrm{\mathrm{Mg}^{2+} < \mathrm{Na}^{+} < \mathrm{F}^{-} < \mathrm{O}^{-2}}}$
So, the correct option is (1).

question: The number of $\mathrm{Cl}$ atoms ionised in the process $\mathrm{Cl} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cl}^{+}+\mathrm{e}$ by the energy liberated for the process $\mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{e} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$for one Avogadro number of atoms is ${\rm{x}} \times {10^{23}}.$ Given $\mathrm{IE}=$ $13.0\,\,{\rm{eV}}$ and ${{\rm{E}}_{\rm{g}}} = 3.60\,\,{\rm{eV}}.$ The value of x is ____.
explanation:
Let $\mathrm{n}$ atoms be ionised
$6.02 \times 10^{23} \times \mathrm{Eg}=\mathrm{n} \times \mathrm{IE}$
${\rm{n}} = \frac{{6.02 \times {{10}^{23}} \times 3.60}}{{13}} = 1.667 \times {10^{23}}$
$\therefore {\rm{x}} = 1.67$

question: The electronegativity of these following elements increases in the order
explanation:
Si and P are in the 3rd period while C and N are in the 2nd period. Elements in 2nd period have higher electronegativities than those in the 3rd period. Since N has smaller size and has higher nuclear charge than C, its electronegativity is higher than that of C. Similarly, the electronegativity of P is higher
than that of Si. Thus, the overall order is :
Si, P, C, N.

question: Which of the following species has the highest electron affinity?
explanation: Higher the energy released in the process of accepting an extra electron, the higher will be the electron affinity. Therefore, halogens have the highest electron affinity. Thus, F has the highest electron affinity.

question: Which one of the following ions has the highest value of ionic radius?
explanation: $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ has the highest value of ionic radius and this can be explained on the basis of Z/e (nuclear charge/ number of electrons) ratio. When $\mathrm{Z} / \mathrm{e}$ ratio increases, the size decreases and when vice-versa $\dfrac{\mathrm{Z}}{\mathrm{e}} \propto \dfrac{1}{\mathrm{r}}$

question: The first ionisation energies of the elements of the first transition series $(\mathrm{Ti} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}$ )
explanation: The first ionisation energies of the elements of $3 \mathrm{~d}$-transition series increases as the atomic number increases. The increasing ionisation enthalpies are due to increased nuclear charge with increase in atomic number that reduces the size of the atom making the removal of outer electron difficult.

decrease as the atomic number increases
do not show any change as the addition of electrons takes place in the inner (n-1)d orbitals
increase from $\mathrm{Ti}$ to $\mathrm{Mn}$ and then decrease from Mn to Cu

atomic size $\propto \dfrac{1}{\text { atomic number }}$

question: Which represents the correct order of first ionisation potential of third period elements?
explanation:
Generally, ionisation potential increases, as we move from left to right in a period. However, the ionisation potential of II A group elements is higher than that of the III A group elements. This is because of the fully-filled $\mathrm{s}$-orbitals in II A group elements.
Hence, the correct order of ionisation potential is
$\mathrm{Na} < \mathrm{Al} < \mathrm{Mg} < \mathrm{Si}$

question: Among the following, the third ionisation energy is highest for
explanation:
The electronic configuration of the given elements is as follows:
$_{{\rm{12}}}{\rm{Mg}} = {\rm{[Ne]3}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\mkern 1mu} _{\rm{5}}}\,{\rm{B}} = {\rm{[He]2}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\rm{,2}}{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{1}}}$
$_{\rm{4}}{\rm{Be}} = {\rm{[He]2}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {{\mkern 1mu} _{{\rm{13}}}}{\rm{Al}} = [{\rm{Ne]3}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\rm{,3}}{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{1}}}$
From the electronic configuration, it is clear that third ionisation energy of $\mathrm{Mg}$ and $\mathrm{Be}$ is higher than that of $\mathrm{Al}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ In them, the third electron has to be removed from the stable inert gas configuration.
Between $\mathrm{Mg}$ and, Be third IE of Be is high because of its smaller size than Mg as both lie in the same group.

question: Which of the following has the highest electron affinity?
explanation: $\mathrm{F}^{-}$and $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ ions have stable electronic configuration, thus they will not accept further electrons, while $\mathrm{O}$ needs electrons to achieve stable arrangement, thus it has highest electron affinity. Further, $\mathrm{Na}$ is a metal, thus it does not have any electron affinity.

question: Which ion has the largest radius?
explanation:
On moving down the group, atomic radius increases, while it decreases on moving from left to right. However, for isoelectronic species, the atomic radius decreases with increase in atomic number. Further, larger the charge on the anion, greater is the radius.
Hence, on considering all the factors, $\mathrm{Se}^{2-}$ would possesses largest ionic radius among the given options.

question:
Which of the following is correct option?
(P) Same number of electrons in d-subshell $(\mathrm{Mn}, \mathrm{Cr}$ )
(Q) Same value of ${{\rm{Z}}_{{\rm{eff }}}}\left( {{\rm{L}}{{\rm{i}}^{\rm{ + }}}\,{\rm{and}}\,\,{{\rm{B}}^{\rm{ + }}}} \right)$
(R) Same value of shielding constant, ${\text{(}}\,{\text{L}}{{\text{i}}^ + }\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,{\text{B}}{{\text{e}}^{{\text{2 + }}}})$
(S) Same value of electronegativity ( P and H )
(T) Same value of electron affinity ( F and Cl )
explanation:
$\mathrm{Mn} \rightarrow[\mathrm{Ar}] 4 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 3 \mathrm{~d}^{5}, \mathrm{Cr} \rightarrow[\mathrm{Ar}] 4 \mathrm{~s}^{1} 3 \mathrm{~d}^{5}$,
So d-electrons in Mn and $\mathrm{Cr}=5$.
$\mathrm{Li}^{+}$and $\mathrm{B}^{+} \rightarrow$ different $\mathrm{Z}_{\text {eff }}$
(inner shell $\mathrm{e}^{-}$different)
$\mathrm{Li}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Be}^{2+} \rightarrow$ same value of screening constant, $\sigma$ as $\sigma_{\text {net }}$ inner shell electrons are same
P and H both have same value of electronegativity.
EA of $\mathrm{Cl}(-349 \mathrm{~kJ} /$ mole $) \& \mathrm{~F}(-333 \mathrm{~kJ} /$ mole $)$.
$\therefore$ (P),(R), and (S) only correct.

question: $\mathrm{N}_{0} / 2$ atoms of $\mathrm{X}(\mathrm{g})$ are converted into $\mathrm{X}^{+}(\mathrm{g})$ by energy $\mathrm{E}_{1} \cdot \mathrm{N}_{0} / 2$ atoms of $\mathrm{X}(\mathrm{g})$ are converted into $\mathrm{X}^{-}(\mathrm{g})$ of energy $\mathrm{E}_{2}$. Hence, ionization energy and electron affinity of $\mathrm{X}(\mathrm{g})$ are:
explanation:
Let $\mathrm{IE}=\mathrm{I}$ and $\mathrm{EA}=\mathrm{E}$
$\mathrm{X}(\mathrm{g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{X}^{+}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{e}$
$\frac{{{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{0}}}}}{2} \times {\rm{I}} = {{\rm{E}}_1}\quad \therefore {\rm{I}} = \frac{{2{{\rm{E}}_1}}}{{\,{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{0}}}}}$
$\mathrm{X}^{+}(\mathrm{g}) \xrightarrow{\mathrm{e}^{-}} \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{g})$
$\frac{{{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{0}}}{\rm{E}}}}{2} = {{\rm{E}}_2}\quad \therefore {\rm{E}} = \frac{{2{{\rm{E}}_2}}}{{\,{{\rm{N}}_{\rm{0}}}}}$
So $\dfrac{2 \mathrm{E}_{1}}{\mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{0}}}, \dfrac{2 \mathrm{E}_{2}}{\mathrm{~N}_{\mathrm{0}}}$ is correct

explanation:
Radius of an atom follows the following order: vander Waals' radii $>$ Metallic radii $>$ Covalent radii
So, both the statements are correct.

options.text: Values of vander Waals' radii are larger than those of covalent radii because the van der Waals' forces are much weaker than the forces operating between atoms in a covalently bonded molecule.
The metallic radii are smaller than the vander Waals' radii, since the bonding forces in the metallic crystal lattice are much stronger than the van der Waals' forces.

question:
Arrange the following elements in increasing order of first ionization enthalpy:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{Li}, \mathrm{Be}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{N}}}$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
explanation: The penetration of a 2 s -electron to the nucleus is more than that of a 2 p -electron; hence the ${\mathrm{2 p}}$ electron of boron is more shielded from the nucleus by the inner core of electrons than the 2 s electrons of beryllium. Therefore, it is easier to remove the ${\mathrm{2 p}}$-electron from boron compared to the removal of a 2 s - electron from beryllium. Thus, boron has a smaller first ionization enthalpy than beryllium. From left to right,i.e, increases. Hence ,the order is ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Li} < \mathrm{B} < \mathrm{Be} < \mathrm{C} < \mathrm{N}}}$.

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully and mark the correct option

Assertion: The first ionization enthalpy of 3d series elements is more than that of group 2 metals.

Reason: In 3d series of elements successive filling of d-orbitals takes place.
explanation:
I.E. of $3 \mathrm{~d}-$ series elements in ${\rm{kJ}}/{\rm{mol}}$
$\mathop {{\rm{Sc}}}\limits_{631} \,\,\mathop {{\rm{Ti}}}\limits_{656} \,\,\mathop {\,{\rm{V}}}\limits_{650} \,\,\mathop {{\rm{Cr}}}\limits_{653} \,\,\mathop {{\rm{Mn}}}\limits_{717} \,\,\mathop {{\rm{Fe}}}\limits_{762} \,\,\mathop {{\rm{Co}}}\limits_{758} \,\,\mathop {{\rm{Ni}}}\limits_{736} \,\,\mathop {{\rm{Cu}}}\limits_{745} \,\,\mathop {{\rm{Zn}}}\limits_{906} $
I.E. values in ${\rm{kJ}}/{\mathop{\rm mol}\nolimits} \,\,of\,\,{2^{{\rm{nd }}}}$ group elements is
$\mathop {{\rm{Be}}}\limits_{899} \,\,\mathop {{\rm{Mg}}}\limits_{737} \,\,\mathop {{\rm{Ca}}}\limits_{590} \,\,\mathop {{\rm{Sr}}}\limits_{549} \,\,\mathop {{\rm{Ba}}}\limits_{503} \,\,\mathop {{\rm{Ra}}}\limits_{509} $
From Sc to Mn , I.E. values are less than that of ${\rm{Mg}}({\rm{Be}}$, a non-metal need not to be considered).
So, Assertion is false. Reason is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.

Assertion: For hydrogen like species, energy of an electron in a particular orbit decreases with increase in value of ${\text{Z}}$.

Reason: Electronegativity decreases across a period.
explanation:
For hydrogen-like species, the energy of an electron in the nth orbit is determined by the equation:
$\mathrm{E}=\dfrac{-13.6 \times \mathrm{Z}^{2}}{\mathrm{n}^{2}}$
Electronegativity increases across a period. So option (3) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Helium has the highest value of ionisation energy among all the elements known.


Reason: Helium has the highest value of electron affinity among all the elements known.
explanation: Helium (He) contains a fully filled ${\text{1}}{{\text{s}}^{\text{2}}}$  orbital, which has a more penetrating effect and is positioned very close to the nucleus. This proximity to the nucleus is the reason helium has the highest value of ionization energy among the elements. Chlorine has the highest electron affinity not helium. So option (3) is correct.

Reason: N, O, F belong to same period of the periodic table.
explanation: Nitrogen has an highly stable half filled configuration of $1 \mathrm{~s}^{2}, 2 \mathrm{~s}^{2}, 2 \mathrm{p}^{3}$ and addition of an electron to it is not easy. But oxygen has a greater tendency to add electron as its p orbital is incomplete $\left(1 s^{2}, 2 s^{2}, 2 p^{4}\right)$. Easier the addition of an electron to the atom, more negative the electron gain enthalpy. N, O, F belong to $2^{\text {nd }}$ period of the periodic table. but there is no relation between these two statements. So, option (2) is correct.

question: Which pair of elements has maximum electronegativity difference?
explanation:
Electronegativity decreases down the group and increases on moving from left to right in a period. Thus, $\mathrm{Li}$ is more electronegative than $\mathrm{Na}$, and $\mathrm{F}$ is more electronegative than $\mathrm{Cl}$ and $\mathrm{Br} . \mathrm{Br}$ is less electronegative than $\mathrm{Cl}$ because of its larger size. Also, lesser be the electronegativity of metal element and more is the electronegativity of halogen atom, then more will be the electronegativity difference of a given species.
Thus, $\mathrm{Na}$ and $\mathrm{F}$ shows the maximum electronegativity difference option (2) correct.

Thus, ${{\rm{O}}^{{\rm{2 - }}}}$ will show most positive ${\rm{\Delta }}{{\rm{H}}_{{\rm{eg}}}}$, while ${{\rm{O}}^{{\rm{2 + }}}}$ will show most negative value due to small size.

question: Which of the following orders of ionic radii is correctly represented?
explanation:
All the options (b,c and d) are incorrect
${\rm{(1)}}\,\,{{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ - }}}{\rm{ > }}{{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}}{\rm{ > H}}$
It is known that radius of a cation is always smaller than that of a neutral atom due to decrease in the number of orbits. Whereas the radius of anion is always greater than a cation due to decrease in effective nuclear charge. Hence the correct order is
${{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ - }}}{\rm{ > }}{{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}}{\rm{ > H}}$
${\rm{(2)}}\,\,{\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}^{\rm{ + }}}{\rm{ > }}{{\rm{F}}^{\rm{ - }}}{\rm{ > }}{{\rm{O}}^{{\rm{2 - }}}}$
The given species are isoelectronic and they are in contact with the same number of electrons. For isoelectronic species,
${\rm{Ionic}}\,\,{\rm{radii}}\,\, \propto \,\,\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{{\rm{atomic}}\,\,{\rm{number}}}}$
$\therefore $ The correct order of ionic radii is
${{\rm{O}}^{{\rm{2 - }}}}{\rm{ > }}{{\rm{F}}^{\rm{ - }}}{\rm{ > N}}{{\rm{a}}^{\rm{ + }}}$
(3) Similarly, the correct option is
${{\rm{O}}^{{\rm{2 - }}}}{\rm{ > }}{{\rm{F}}^{\rm{ - }}}{\rm{ > N}}{{\rm{a}}^{\rm{ + }}}$
(4) The correct order is ${{\rm{N}}^{{\rm{3 - }}}}{\rm{ > M}}{{\rm{g}}^{{\rm{2 + }}}}{\rm{ > A}}{{\rm{l}}^{{\rm{3 + }}}}$

question: Which halogen has the highest value of negative electron gain enthalpy?
explanation:
Halogens have highest negative electron gain enthalpy in their respective periods, because they have only one electron less than stable noble gas configurations. As a result, they readily accept one electron to acquire the noble gas configuration. Electron gain enthalpy becomes less negative on moving from chlorine to iodine. However, negative electron gain enthalpy of fluorine is less than that of chlorine due to small sized of fluorine atom. It has very high inter electronic repulsion in the relatively small 2p_x0002_orbitals. Hence, incoming electron experience less attraction from the nucleus.
$\therefore $ Chlorine has the highest value of electron gain enthalpy.

explanation:
Generally, the ionisation enthalpies or energy increases from left to right in a period and decreases from top to bottom in a group. Several factors such as atomic radius, nuclear charge, shielding effect are responsible for change of ionisation enthalpies.

question: The first ionisation enthalpies of Na, Mg, Al and Si are in the order:
explanation:
Na, Mg, Al and Si belongs to the same period i.e., 3rd period.
I.P. increases as we move along a period from left to right due to decrease in atomic size.
Hence, the trend in I.P. should be Si > Al > Mg > Na
However, it is seen that Mg has higher ionization potential than Al because of its stable electronic configuration.
So, the true order of first I.P. is Na < Mg > Al < Si.

question: The correct order of electron gain enthalpy with negative sign of F, Cl, Br and I, having atomic number 9, 17, 35 and 53 respectively, is :
explanation: As we move down in a group electron gain enthalpy becomes less negative because the size of the atom increases and the distance of added electron from the nucleus increases. Negative electron gain enthalpy of F is less than Cl. This is due to the fact that when an electron is added to F, the added electron goes to the smaller n = 2 energy level and experiences significant repulsion from the other electrons present in this level. In Cl, the electron goes to the larger n = 3 energy level and consequently occupies a larger region of space leading to much less electron - electron repulsion. So the correct order is Cl > F > Br > I.

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct options given below:

Assertion: The electronic configuration of the elements determines their position in the periodic table.

Reason: Elements are arranged in the periodic table in order of increasing atomic number, which corresponds to their number of protons and overall electronic configuration.
explanation: The position of an element in the periodic table is based on its atomic number, which directly determines the element's electronic   configuration. This configuration influences the element's chemical properties and thus its placement in the periodic table.  Correct option is (1).

Assertion is true, but Reason is false.
Assertion is  false  but reason is  true.

question: Match the following.
explanation:
$\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{S} ;{ }_{56} \mathrm{Ba}=[\mathrm{Xe}] 6 \mathrm{~s}^{2}$
So, group number $=2$; period number $=6$
$\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{R} ;{ }_{50} \mathrm{Sn}=[\mathrm{Kr}] 4 \mathrm{~d}^{10} 55^{2} 5 \mathrm{p}^{2}$
Hence, period number $=5$; group number $=10+4=14$
$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{P} ;{ }_{27} \mathrm{Co}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{7} 4 \mathrm{~s}^{2}$
Hence, period number $=4$; group number $=7+2=9$
$\mathrm{D}-\mathrm{Q} ;{ }_{5 \mathrm{~s}} \mathrm{Ce}=[\mathrm{Xe}] 4 \mathrm{f}^{1} 5 \mathrm{~d}^{1} 6 \mathrm{~s}^{2}$
Hence, period number $=6 ;$ group number $=3$

question: Match column I with column II.
explanation:
(A-S) $\mathrm{ns}^{2} \mathrm{np}^{5}$ is general valence shell electron configuration of halogens. So this configuration belongs to bromine.
(B-Q) $(\mathrm{n}-1) \mathrm{d}^{10} \mathrm{~ns}^{1}$ is for copper $\left([\mathrm{Ar}] 4 \mathrm{~s}^{1} 3 \mathrm{~d}^{10}\right)$
(C-P) $(\mathrm{n}-1) \mathrm{d}^{5} \mathrm{~ns}^{1}$ configuration belongs to chromium. $\left([\mathrm{Ar}] 4 \mathrm{~s}^{1} 3 \mathrm{~d}^{5}\right)$
(D-R) Noble gases has valence shell electron configuration ${\rm{(n}} - {\rm{1)}}{{\rm{d}}^{1 - 10}}$ $\mathrm{ns}^{2} \mathrm{np}^{6}$, so it belongs to krypton.

The elements having ${\rm{1}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}\,{\rm{2}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}\,{\rm{2}}{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{6}}}\,{\rm{3}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}\,\,{\rm{and}}\,\,{\rm{1}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}\,{\rm{2}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}$ configuration belong to same period.

explanation:
${\rm{2}}{{\rm{p}}^{\rm{6}}}{\rm{,}}\,{\rm{3}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}} \to $ Belongs to 3rd period
${\rm{1}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\rm{,}}\,{\rm{2}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}} \to $ Belongs to 2nd period
Both have ${\rm{n}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\rm{,}}$ i.e. same electronic configuration. Hence both belongs to different period. So, the option (2) is correct.

explanation:

${\rm{B}}{{\rm{e}}^{\rm{ + }}}{\rm{ = 1}}{{\rm{s}}^{{\rm{2}}\,}}{\rm{2}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}$ 2s-electron is screened by two electrons in 1s-orbital i.e., (n-1) th orbital)

${{\rm{Z}}_{{\rm{eff}}}}{\rm{ = 4 - 1}}{\rm{.70 = 2}}{\rm{.30}}$

question:
Assertion: Shielding effect increases as we go down the group.

Reason: More is the number of electrons in the penultimate shell, more is shielding.
explanation: The decrease in the effective nuclear charge on the valence electrons due to the electrons in the penultimate shell is called shielding effect. This effect increases as the number of penultimate electrons increases. So option (1) is correct.
